title: Dormant Account Reactivation via Credential Stuffing with Geographic Shift
narrative: Account shows zero activity for 90+ days, then abruptly executes 3-8 transactions within 72 hours totaling $800-$2,400. The reactivation coincides with email domain change from corporate (company.com) to generic webmail (P_emaildomain switches to gmail/yahoo). New transactions originate from dist1 values 500+ km from historical addr1, often in different timezones. Fraudster tests with $15-$40 digital goods (ProductCD='W') in first 6 hours, then escalates to $200-$400 electronics if successful. Card1_txn_count_1day jumps from 0 to 5-7. Original cardholder's pattern was daytime purchases (txn_hour 9-17), but takeover shows is_night=1 clustering. No failed login attempts visible in transaction data, suggesting valid credentials obtained from breach lists.
indicators: Account dormancy period exceeds 90 days with sudden burst of 3-8 transactions in 72-hour window
P_emaildomain changes from corporate/ISP domain to gmail.com, yahoo.com, or outlook.com during reactivation
dist1 geographic distance exceeds 500 km from historical addr1 pattern
Initial test transactions $15-$40 for digital goods (ProductCD='W') followed by $200-$400+ physical goods within 24 hours
Shift to nighttime activity (is_night=1, txn_hour 22-04) contradicting historical daytime pattern (txn_hour 9-17)
card1_amt_zscore exceeds 3.5 compared to historical transaction amounts
summary: A long-dormant account suddenly springs to life with multiple transactions from a new geographic location 500+ km away, paired with an email address change from corporate to generic webmail. The spending pattern shows small digital goods purchases followed rapidly by higher-value electronics, all occurring at unusual nighttime hours.
reasoning: Attackers use stolen credentials from data breaches to access inactive accounts that legitimate owners aren't monitoring. They test the account with low-value digital purchases to confirm the card works, then quickly escalate to expensive items before the victim notices, exploiting the window before fraud detection triggers.
indicator_explanations: Account sits unused for months then suddenly explodes with multiple purchases
Email switches from legitimate provider to free webmail service during comeback
Purchases originate from location hundreds of miles from cardholder's normal area
Attacker tests with cheap digital items then quickly escalates to expensive goods
Card goes from zero activity to flurry of purchases in single day
Transactions happen late night when cardholder historically shops during business hours
Purchase amounts are dramatically larger than cardholder's typical spending
